# Stage 3: Write Copy That Sounds Human, Not AI

**This is the stage where most build-your-own systems quietly die.** You wired up the data, the sending infrastructure works, and then the copy reads like it was generated by a machine that has never had a real conversation. Buyers feel it in half a second. So this section is the deepest one in the whole guide, because getting the words right is worth more than any other lever you own.

### The core tension you have to resolve first

Here is the paradox we keep running into. Across millions of sends, personalized AI copy still beats generic no-personalization copy on both reply rate and positive sentiment. Personalization works. *And yet* the specific move everyone learned first, feeding a company description to a model and asking for a clever first line, is now dying fast. Buyers recognize generic AI instantly. The average prospect gets roughly **10 near-identical AI emails a day**, all opening with the same "I noticed your company is doing X" shape.

So the answer is not "stop personalizing" and it is not "personalize harder." The resolution is three moves stacked together:

1. **Segment more than you personalize.** Sort prospects into tight buckets and write genuinely strong copy per bucket.
2. **Lead with a real researched signal**, not a paraphrase of their homepage.
3. **Do a light human pass** on every AI draft, fixing only the lines a real person would never say out loud.

Hold those three in your head. Everything below is in service of them.

### The mindset shifts that change your copy

Before templates, you need to think differently, because copy is downstream of belief.

- **You are losing to their comfort, not to a competitor.** Your prospect's real "vendor" is the status quo. Your job is to make staying put feel more expensive than changing. Frame the cost of doing nothing, not the shine of your solution.
- **Sort, do not sell.** Lean back. You are looking for who is a fit, not begging everyone to say yes. When you write from scarcity, you get what we call *commission breath*, that faint smell of neediness that makes buyers pull away. Write like you have a full pipeline, even before you do.
- **Resonance beats personalization.** Showing you understand their *world* beats "I saw you went to State." A line that names the exact operational headache their role feels every Monday will out-pull a scraped trivia fact every time.
- **The outlier mandate.** Do not ideate from the model's defaults. If your line reads like the 40th version of that line they have seen this quarter, it is dead on arrival, no matter how "personalized" it technically is.

### The Poke-the-Bear question

The single most reliable opener we teach is a **neutral, non-leading question that surfaces a blind spot** in how they currently do things. It works because it does not sell, it makes them think.

Contrast the two:

- *Commission breath (leading):* "Wouldn't you love to book more meetings this quarter?" (Obvious agenda. They stop reading.)
- *Poke-the-Bear (neutral):* "How are you keeping reply rates up now that everyone's inbox is flooded with the same AI intros?"

The second one is honest, it names a real tension, and it does not presume the answer. Structure it like this:

```
How are you [handling / measuring / keeping up with]
[a hard, current problem in their world]
now that [a recent shift that makes the old way harder]?
```

> 💡 A good poke question is one they cannot answer with a confident "we've got that covered." If it is easy to dismiss, it is not poking anything.

### An angle menu to pull from

Never open every email the same way. Keep this menu nearby and choose the angle that fits the segment:

- **Poke-the-bear**: the neutral blind-spot question above.
- **Problem-sniffing**: name a symptom you suspect, ask if it is real for them.
- **Three tailored ideas**: "here are 3 things I'd try for [their situation]," value up front.
- **Look-alike / social proof**: "we did X for a company that looks a lot like yours."
- **Founder story**: a short, human why-we-built-this that earns a reply.
- **Channel displacement**: "you're leaning hard on [channel], here's a cheaper channel doing the same job."
- **Cost of the status quo**: quantify what doing nothing is costing them monthly.
- **Their own reviews / complaints**: quote a real review or support gripe and offer the fix.
- **Reverse lead magnet**: give them a useful asset first, ask for nothing.

### The body scaffold

Teach yourself this line-by-line and you will never stare at a blank draft again.

```
[Situation recognition]: one real signal that proves you looked.
[Value prop + proof]: what you do for people like them, with a number.
[Optional: "specifically for you"]: one line tying it to their exact case.
[Low-effort CTA]: a tiny yes, not a calendar demand.
[Optional P.S.]: a soft offer, a proof point, or a bit of personality.
```

Notice the CTA is *low-effort*. You are asking for a nod, not a 30-minute commitment on the first touch.

### The discipline rules

These are non-negotiable once you are editing.

- **Recipient-to-sender word ratio of at least 3:1.** Count the "you / your / their world" words against the "we / I / our" words. Make it about them, not you.
- **No em dashes.** This is the number one AI tell in cold email right now. Models lean on the em dash constantly, and human sales reps almost never type one. Use a period, a comma, or the word "and." Kill every em dash in your drafts on sight.
- **No buzzwords, no "hope this finds you well," no perfectly-parallel triads.** Real people do not write "streamline, optimize, and accelerate." That tidy rhythm is a fingerprint.
- **The read-out-loud test.** Read the email aloud. If you would never say a sentence to a human's face, cut it.
- **The outlier test.** Would this line surprise them, or is it the 40th version? If it is the 40th, rewrite it.
- **Keep a saturation list.** This is your own running log of phrases that now scream "templated" because models default to phrasing that scored well 12 to 24 months ago. Build it yourself by reading your own inbox: every time three cold emails use the same phrase, add it to the list and ban it from your drafts. We are teaching you the *habit*, not handing you a list, because the list goes stale in months and yours will always be fresher.

> ⚠️ A saturation list you copied from someone else is already out of date. The value is in maintaining your own.

### The AI-tell checklist

Run every draft against this before it sends:

```
[ ] The 4-sentence "ChatGPT default" shape (intro, one middle, one value, CTA)
[ ] Three suspiciously generic bullet points
[ ] Two or three personalization tokens stacked into line 1
[ ] Any em dash, anywhere
[ ] Over-formal cadence a rep would never speak aloud
```

Then do a **light second-pass edit**: fix only the lines a human would never say, and leave the rest alone. You are not rewriting the email, you are removing the tells.

### Segmentation beats personalization

Here is the reframe that scales. Instead of asking AI for a shallow unique line per person, use AI to **classify prospects into buckets** (by trigger, by role, by pain), then write one genuinely strong email per bucket. Ten great segment emails beat a thousand mediocre "unique" ones.

> 📌 In our testing, real offers beat asks. Including a real, concrete offer lifts replies by about **+28%**, while opening with a direct meeting ask can cut replies by about **-44%** (from an 85M-email analysis, directional: verify for your setup). Emails of **50 to 125 words** get roughly **2.4x** the reply rate of 200+ word emails (directional: verify for your setup).

> 📌 One campaign we ran hit about **14% reply with essentially zero personalization**, because the *offer and the segment* were exactly right. The subject line was literally the offer. Proof that targeting and offer beat clever lines every time.

### Subject lines

Keep them short, lowercase, and either curiosity-forward or offer-forward. Lowercase subject lines beat Title Case by about **21%** in a 12M-email test, and **4 to 5 words** is the sweet spot (directional: verify for your setup). Fill-in formulas:

```
1. quick idea for [their team / their goal]
2. [their metric] without [the painful part]
3. re: [their trigger event]
4. [free asset] for [their role]
5. worth a look? [the offer in 2 words]
```

Write the offer into the subject, keep it lowercase, and let the body earn the reply.
